moment_text: Sure. Well, when I was at WashU, I was a psych major and I thought I wanted to be a clinical psychologist, but what I was finding out was that really, um, those. Uh, programs at the time were few and far between. They were really focused on research. So long story short, I decided to go to medical school with some support from other folks, and I did that. I made that decision my senior year of college. So I, uh, graduated and did all my pre-med in a year and, and, um, took the mca, interviewed and luckily got into medical school, university of Texas, hook 'em, horns, and, uh. I, um, did my resident, yeah, sorry. I did my, um, rotation in psychiatry, which I really loved, but I found myself more of a generalist and wanting to kind of integrate. So I decided that I wanted to do my residency in general internal medicine with the knowledge and belief that mental health is as much a part of that as, you know, your physical health. Um, and really we shouldn't be. Dissecting or, or dividing the two, they really are integrated. Um, our mental health comes from a lot of physiology. So in any case, um, being a primary care physician is what, uh, you know, allows me to do that. And then in practice, uh, kind of fast forward when I was at UCLA and decided to really focus on women's health, you know, it. Became even more apparent, um, in terms of the integration and the effect of, um, our hormones, our external circumstances on women's mental health, and then in turn how that mental health can impact us with, you know, again, just having chronic stress, increasing our cortisol inclu, increasing our inflammation, and then leading to all sorts of chronic diseases. So, you know, I think it was kind of a. Full circle, um, in that I started in psych, but I, I came around to kind of integrating it, uh, more holistically.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate with both agency signals (making decisions) and barrier signals (fragmented care). It describes a foundational realization about the need to integrate mental and physical health, challenging a systemic norm.

moment_text: You know, I think part of it is just being comfortable speaking about different things and you know, this may or may not be a little tangential when we were trying to. Help other physicians understand the importance of women's health in primary care. Um, I was asked to do a talk, and the talk that I gave was on domestic violence. And the reason I chose domestic violence as a women's health issue is because it really did integrate not only physical abuse. Some of the challenges around control of another person, but also the mental stresses and the effects of those mental stresses on, on health, and you know, it's much more than that, but also the effects of trauma. On, especially women. And I think that's a big issue for so many women, unfortunately, whether it be, um, trauma, sexual abuse, violence, you know, unfortunately, again, um, we're more, um, at risk for that. And so just really being able to. Have those discussions and ask those questions. I think, you know, certainly, you know, my experience in psychology and psychiatry helped me. Um, I have personal and family experience that I think I brought into it a bit. Um, and then increasing work that was being done to show the. Real connections between things like fibromyalgia and um, and trauma or obesity or eating disorders and, and trauma. Um, again, those are very physical issues that. End up, um, you know, maybe being triggered by those early, uh, experiences and mental health experiences, if you will. And, you know, just going back to the whole issue about stress, you know, the effect of cortisol on heart disease, blood pressure, diabetes, uh, as well as certainly, um, you know, anxiety and depression.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate by containing both barrier signals (education_gap, stigma) and agency signals (speaking_up). It details a specific action taken to address a systemic knowledge gap in medical training regarding trauma and women's health.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker mentions sexual abuse and violence as risk factors for women, which warrants the risk flags.

moment_text: Well, I think, you know, when you say discover, I think I, I was. Um, and what we wanted to do again, because we were internist and still are, uh, we wanted to focus on. All of the ways that sex and gender impact on our health, whether it's cardio, you know, let me kind of back up. We think about health with those, uh, conditions that manifest differently in women like heart disease, uh, predominantly in women Alzheimer's, osteoporosis, autoimmune diseases, and then obviously the reproductive and gynecological issues. And so we really wanted to address those. Um. With women, um, with the literature that was already there, but also that was growing and that some of our researchers were actually already doing. And so to be able to do that and with that was the understanding that mental health is a part of that. It's, um. Part of our lived experience, it's part of, again, impacting on health. Uh, maybe we didn't know all the little details that we know now, but we certainly knew that it did. And to be able to create, um, an approach to primary care for women that. Understood that, acknowledged it and, you know, uh, treated it, you know, where it needed to be was really kind of, exci was definitely exciting.
